302	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
303	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
304	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
305	  \mathlibok
306	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
307	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
308	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
309	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
310	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
311	\end{lemma}
312	
313	\begin{lemma}
314	
315	
316	\leanok
317	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
318	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
320	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
321	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
322	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
323	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
324	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
325	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
326	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
327	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
328	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
329	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
330	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
331	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
332	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
333	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
334	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
335	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{proof}
339	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
340	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
341	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
342	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
343	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
344	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
345	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
346	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
347	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
348	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
349	\end{proof}
350	
351	\begin{lemma}\leanok
352	[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
353	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
355	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator,
356	        lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
357	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
358	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
359	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
360	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
361	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
362	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
363	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
364	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
365	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
366	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
367	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
368	  normalisation with denominators).}
369	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
370	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
371	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
372	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
373	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
374	  \[
375	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
376	  \]
377	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
378	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
379	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
380	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
381	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
382	\end{lemma}
383	
384	\begin{proof}
385	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
386	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
387	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
388	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
389	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
390	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
391	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
392	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
393	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
394	
395	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
396	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
397	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
398	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
399	  \(x \in \sigma\), a standard integrality-and-localization descent argument yields a
400	  non-zero \(a_x \in A\) such that \(a_x \cdot (1 \otimes x)\)
401	  is integral over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
402	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
403	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
404	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
405	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
406	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
407	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the proof discharges a whole generator
408	  at once using this integrality-descent step, folding that primitive over the coefficients
409	  internally.)
410	
411	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
412	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
413	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
414	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
415	
416	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
417	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
418	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
419	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
420	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
421	  \[
422	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
423	  \]
424	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
425	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
426	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
427	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
428	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
429	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
430	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
431	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
432	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
433	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
434	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
435	
436	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
437	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
438	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
439	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
440	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
441	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
442	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
443	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
444	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
445	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
446	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
447	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
448	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
449	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
450	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
451	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
452	
453	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
454	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
455	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
456	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
457	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
458	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
459	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
460	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
461	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
462	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
463	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
464	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
465	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
466	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
467	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
468	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
469	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
470	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
471	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
475	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
476	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
478	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
479	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
480	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
481	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
482	\end{lemma}
483	\begin{proof}
484	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
485	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
486	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
487	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
488	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
489	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
490	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
491	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
492	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
493	\end{proof}
494	
495	\begin{lemma}
496	
497	
498	\leanok
499	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
500	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
502	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
503	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
504	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
505	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
506	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
507	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
508	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
509	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
510	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
511	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
512	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
513	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
514	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
515	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
516	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
517	  \[
518	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
519	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
520	  \]
521	
522	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
523	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
524	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
525	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
526	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
527	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{proof}
531	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
532	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
533	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
534	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
535	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
536	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
537	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
538	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
539	
540	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
541	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
542	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
543	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
544	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
545	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
546	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
547	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
548	
549	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
550	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
551	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
552	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
553	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
554	\end{proof}
555	
556	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
557	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
558	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
559	  \mathlibok
560	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
561	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
562	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
563	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
564	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
565	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
566	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
567	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
568	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
569	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
570	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
571	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
572	\end{lemma}
573	
574	\begin{lemma}\leanok
575	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
576	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
578	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
579	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
580	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
581	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
582	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
583	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
584	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
585	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
586	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
587	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
588	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
589	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
590	\end{lemma}
591	
592	\begin{proof}
593	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
594	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
595	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
596	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
597	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
598	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
599	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
600	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
601	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
602	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
603	\end{proof}

605	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
606	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
607	
608	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
609	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
610	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
611	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
612	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
613	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
614	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
615	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
616	
617	\begin{definition}\leanok
618	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
619	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
621	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
622	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
623	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
624	  \[
625	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
626	  \]
627	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
628	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
629	\end{definition}
630	
631	\begin{lemma}\leanok
632	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
633	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
635	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
636	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
637	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
641	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
642	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
643	\end{proof}
644	
645	\begin{definition}\leanok
646	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
647	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
649	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
650	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
651	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
652	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
653	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
654	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
655	\end{definition}
656	
657	\begin{lemma}\leanok
658	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
659	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
660	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
661	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
662	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
666	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
667	\end{proof}
668	
669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
671	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
673	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
674	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
675	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
676	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
677	\end{lemma}
678	\begin{proof}
679	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
680	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
681	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
682	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
683	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
684	\end{proof}
685	
686	\begin{lemma}\leanok
687	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
688	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
689	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
690	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
691	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
692	  transformed monomial is the radix value
693	  \[
694	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
695	  \]
696	\end{lemma}
697	\begin{proof}
698	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
699	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
700	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
701	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
702	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
703	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
704	\end{proof}
705	
706	\begin{lemma}\leanok
707	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
708	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
709	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
710	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
711	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
712	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
713	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
714	\end{lemma}
715	\begin{proof}
716	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
717	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
718	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
719	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
720	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
721	\end{proof}
722	
723	\begin{lemma}\leanok
724	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
725	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
726	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
727	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
728	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
729	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
730	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
731	\end{lemma}
732	\begin{proof}
733	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
734	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
735	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
736	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
737	\end{proof}
738	
739	\begin{lemma}\leanok
740	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
741	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
742	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
743	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
744	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
745	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
746	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
747	\end{lemma}
748	\begin{proof}
749	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
750	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
751	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
752	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
753	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
754	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
755	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
756	\end{proof}
757	
758	\begin{lemma}\leanok
759	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
760	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
761	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
762	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
763	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
764	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
765	\end{lemma}
766	\begin{proof}
767	  By induction on \(q\): the isomorphism is additive and multiplicative, and agrees with
768	  \(\varphi\) on constants and variables, so it commutes with \(\varphi\) on all polynomials.
769	\end{proof}
770	
771	\begin{lemma}\leanok
772	[Constant inclusion under the variable-splitting isomorphism]
773	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
774	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
775	  For \(s \in R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), the composite of the inclusion
776	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) (sending \(X_j \mapsto X_j\))
777	  with the isomorphism \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) sends \(s\)
778	  to the constant polynomial \(s \in (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\).
779	\end{lemma}
780	\begin{proof}
781	  By induction on \(s\): a constant and each variable \(X_j\) (\(j \ge 1\)) map to themselves,
782	  and the isomorphism is an algebra map, so it sends products and sums of such to constants in
783	  the outer polynomial ring.
784	\end{proof}
785	
786	\begin{lemma}\leanok
787	[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
788	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
789	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
790	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
791	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
792	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
793	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
794	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
795	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
796	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
797	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
798	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
799	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
800	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
801	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
802	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
803	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
804	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
805	\end{lemma}
806	
807	\begin{proof}
808	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
809	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
810	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
811	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
812	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
813	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
814	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
815	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
816	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
817	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
818	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
819	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
820	\end{proof}
821	
822	\begin{lemma}[L5b engine anchor — quotient by a monic univariate polynomial is finite]
823	  \label{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite}
824	  \lean{Polynomial.Monic.finite_quotient}
825	  \mathlibok
826	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
827	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in S[X]\) a monic polynomial. Then the quotient
828	  \(S[X]/(f)\) is a module-finite \(S\)-module (indeed free of rank \(\deg f\)).
829	\end{lemma}

831	\begin{lemma}\leanok
832	[L5b.3 — single-variable elimination engine (shared)]
833	  \label{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
834	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_of_monic_lastVar}
835	  \uses{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
836	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
837	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
838	  % the division algorithm in one variable is the standard mechanism of the support drop.
839	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
840	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
841	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
842	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
843	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
844	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
845	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
846	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
847	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
848	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
849	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
850	\end{lemma}
851	
852	\begin{proof}
853	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
854	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
855	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
856	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
857	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
858	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
859	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
860	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
861	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
862	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
863	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
864	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
865	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
866	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
867	\end{proof}
868	
869	\begin{lemma}
870	
871	
872	\leanok
873	[L5b — torsion reindex onto fewer variables]
874	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
875	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_reindex}
876	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
877	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
878	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
879	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
880	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
881	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
882	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
883	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
884	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
885	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
886	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
887	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
888	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
889	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
890	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
891	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
892	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
893	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
894	\end{lemma}
895	
896	\begin{proof}
897	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
898	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
899	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
900	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
901	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}
178	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
179	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
180	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
181	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
182	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
183	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
184	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
185	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
186	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
187	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
188	\end{proof}
189	
190	\begin{definition}
191	
192	\leanok
193	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
194	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
195	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
196	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
197	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
198	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
199	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
200	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
201	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
202	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
203	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
204	  charts. Suppose given:
205	  \begin{itemize}
206	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
207	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
208	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
209	      \[
210	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
211	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
212	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
213	      \]
214	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
215	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
216	  \end{itemize}
217	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
218	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
219	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
220	  \((g_{ij})\):
221	  \begin{enumerate}
222	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
223	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
224	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
225	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
226	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
227	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
228	      isomorphisms satisfy
229	      \[
230	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
231	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
232	      \]
233	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
234	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
235	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
236	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
237	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
238	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
239	      condition.
240	  \end{enumerate}
241	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
242	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
243	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
244	  construction produces:
245	  \begin{enumerate}
246	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
247	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
248	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
249	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
250	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
251	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
252	  \end{enumerate}
253	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
254	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
255	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
256	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
257	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
258	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
259	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
260	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
261	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
262	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
263	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
264	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
265	
266	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
267	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
268	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
269	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
270	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
271	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
272	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
273	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
274	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
275	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
276	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
277	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
278	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
279	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
280	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
281	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
282	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
283	  built.
284	
285	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
286	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
287	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
288	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
289	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
290	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
291	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
292	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
293	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
294	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
295	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
296	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
297	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
298	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
299	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
300	
301	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
302	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
303	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
304	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
305	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
306	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
307	\end{definition}
308	
309	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
310	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
311	
312	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
313	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
314	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
315	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
316	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
317	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
318	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
319	
320	\begin{lemma}[The opens-pullback functor is final]
321	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
322	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
323	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
324	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
325	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
326	\end{lemma}
327	
328	\begin{proof}
329	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
330	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
331	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
332	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
333	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
334	\end{proof}
335	
336	\begin{lemma}[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
337	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
339	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
340	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
341	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
342	  \[
343	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
344	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
345	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
346	  \]
347	\end{lemma}
348	
349	\begin{proof}
350	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
351	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
352	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
353	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
354	\end{proof}
355	
356	\begin{lemma}[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
357	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
358	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
359	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
360	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
361	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
362	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
363	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
364	\end{lemma}
365	
366	\begin{proof}
367	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
368	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
369	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
370	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
371	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
372	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
373	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
374	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
375	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
376	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
377	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
378	\end{proof}
379	
380	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
381	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
382	
383	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
384	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
385	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
386	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
387	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
388	
389	\begin{definition}\leanok
390	[Global section of the unit module]
391	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
392	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
393	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
394	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
395	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
396	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
397	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
398	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
399	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
400	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
401	  global function.
402	\end{definition}
403	
404	\begin{definition}\leanok
405	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
406	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
408	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
409	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
410	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
411	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
412	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
413	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
414	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
415	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
416	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
417	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
418	\end{definition}
419	
420	\begin{lemma}\leanok
421	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
422	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
424	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
425	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
426	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
427	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
428	\end{lemma}
429	
430	\begin{proof}
431	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
432	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
433	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
434	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
435	\end{proof}
436	
437	\begin{lemma}\leanok
438	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
439	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
440	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
441	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
442	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
443	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
444	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
445	\end{lemma}
446	
447	\begin{proof}
448	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
449	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
450	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
451	\end{proof}
452	
453	\begin{definition}
454	
455	\leanok
456	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
457	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
458	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
459	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
460	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
461	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
462	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
463	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
464	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
465	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
466	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
467	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
468	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
469	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
470	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
471	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
472	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
473	  \[
474	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
475	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
476	  \]
477	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
478	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
479	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
480	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
481	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
482	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
483	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
484	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
485	
486	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
487	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
488	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
489	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
490	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
491	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
492	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
493	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
494	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
495	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
496	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
497	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
498	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
499	  free sheaves'' primitive.
500	\end{definition}
501	
502	\begin{lemma}\leanok
503	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
504	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
505	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
506	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
507	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
508	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
509	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
510	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
511	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
512	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
513	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
514	  identity:
515	  \[
516	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
517	  \]
518	\end{lemma}
519	
520	\begin{proof}
521	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
522	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
523	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
524	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
525	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
526	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
527	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
528	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
529	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
530	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
531	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
532	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
533	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
534	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
535	\end{proof}
536	
537	\begin{lemma}\leanok
538	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
539	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
541	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
542	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
543	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
544	\end{lemma}
545	
546	\begin{proof}
547	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
548	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
549	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
550	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
551	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
552	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
553	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
554	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
555	  \[
556	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
557	  \]
558	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
559	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
560	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
561	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
562	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
563	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
564	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
565	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
566	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
567	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
568	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
569	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
570	\end{proof}
571	
572	\begin{definition}
573	
574	\leanok
575	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
576	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
578	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
579	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
580	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
581	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
582	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
583	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
584	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
585	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
586	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
587	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
588	  %  by putting
589	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
590	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
591	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
592	  \[
593	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
594	  \]
595	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
596	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
597	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
598	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
599	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
600	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
601	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
602	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
603	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
604	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
605	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
606	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
607	\end{definition}
608	
609	\begin{definition}
610	
611	\leanok
612	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
613	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
614	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
615	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
616	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
617	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
618	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
619	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
620	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
621	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
622	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
623	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
624	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
625	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
626	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
627	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
628	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
629	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
630	  \[
631	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
632	  \]
633	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
634	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
635	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
636	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
637	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
638	  \[
639	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
640	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
641	  \]
642	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
643	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
644	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
645	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
646	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
647	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
648	\end{definition}
649	
650	\section{The functor of points}
651	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
652	
653	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
654	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
655	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
656	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
657	identities that make that action functorial.
658	
659	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
660	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
661	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
662	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
663	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
664	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
665	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
666	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
667	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
668	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
669	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
670	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
671	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
672	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
673	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
674	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
675	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
676	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
677	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
678	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
679	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
680	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
681	  functor's value at \(T\).
682	
683	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
684	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
685	  \[
686	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
687	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
688	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
689	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
690	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
691	  \]
692	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
693	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
694	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
695	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
696	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
697	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
698	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
699	  map of equivalence classes.
700	\end{definition}
701	
702	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
703	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
704	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
705	
706	\begin{lemma}[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
707	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
708	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
709	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
710	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
711	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
712	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
713	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
714	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
715	\end{lemma}
716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
719	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
720	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
721	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
