722	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
723	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
724	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
725	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
726	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
727	\end{proof}
728	
729	\begin{lemma}[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
730	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
731	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
732	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
733	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
734	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
735	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
736	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
737	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
738	\end{lemma}
739	
740	\begin{proof}
741	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
742	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
743	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
744	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
745	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The pentagon coherence of the
746	  iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation well-defined.
747	\end{proof}
748	
749	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
750	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
751	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
752	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
753	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
754	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
755	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
756	
757	\begin{definition}
758	
759	\leanok
760	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
761	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
762	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
763	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
764	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
765	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
766	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
767	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
768	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
769	  % "represents the
770	  %  contravariant functor
771	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
772	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
773	  %  set of all equivalence classes
774	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
775	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
776	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
777	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
778	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
779	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
780	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
781	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
782	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
783	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
784	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
785	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
786	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
787	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
788	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
789	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
790	  \[
791	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
792	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
793	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
794	  \]
795	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
796	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
797	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
798	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
799	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
800	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
801	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
802	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
803	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
804	\end{definition}
805	
806	\section{The universal property}
807	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
808	
809	\begin{theorem}
810	
811	\leanok
812	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
813	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
814	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
815	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
816	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
817	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
818	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
819	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
820	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
822	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
823	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
824	  %  contravariant functor
825	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
826	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
827	  %  set of all equivalence classes
828	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
829	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
830	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
831	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
832	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
833	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
834	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
835	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
836	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
837	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
838	  \[
839	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
840	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
841	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
842	    \qquad
843	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
844	      \;\longmapsto\;
845	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
846	  \]
847	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
848	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
849	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
850	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
851	  universal family.
852	\end{theorem}
853	
854	\begin{proof}
855	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
856	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
857	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
858	    def:grassmannian_functor}
859	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
860	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
861	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
862	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
863	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
864	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
865	
866	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
867	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
868	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
869	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
870	  the composite
871	  \[
872	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
873	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
874	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
875	  \]
876	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
877	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
878	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
879	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
880	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
881	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
882	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
883	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
884	
885	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
886	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
887	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
888	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
889	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
890	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
891	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
892	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
893	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
894	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
895	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
896	
897	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
898	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
899	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
900	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
901	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
902	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
903	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
904	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
905	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
906	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
907	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
908	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
909	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
910	
911	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
912	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
913	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
914	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
915	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
916	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
917	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
918	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
919	
920	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
921	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
922	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
923	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
924	\end{proof}
925

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
240	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
242	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
243	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
244	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
245	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
246	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
250	[Tensor-power recursion step]
251	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
253	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
254	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
256	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
257	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
262	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
264	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
266	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
267	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
268	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
269	  \[
270	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
271	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
272	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
273	  \]
274	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
275	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
276	  \mathcal{F}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{lemma}\leanok
280	[Zeroth twist]
281	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
283	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
284	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
285	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
286	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
287	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
288	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
289	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
290	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
294	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
295	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
296	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
297	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
298	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
299	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
300	
301	\begin{definition}\leanok
302	[Counit isomorphism of module sheafification]
303	  \label{def:sheafificationCounitIso}
304	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso}
305	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
306	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) the counit of the sheafification
307	  adjunction (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
308	  \cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) gives a canonical isomorphism
309	  \((\mathcal{G})^{\#} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) of sheaves of modules,
310	  invertible because the right adjoint forgetful functor to presheaves of modules
311	  is fully faithful.
312	\end{definition}
313	
314	\begin{definition}\leanok
315	[Left unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
316	  \label{def:tensorObjUnitIso}
317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso}
318	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
319	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
320	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical left-unitor
321	  isomorphism \(\mathbf{1}_X \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
322	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
323	  \cref{def:unitModule}), obtained by sheafifying the presheaf left unitor of
324	  \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} and composing with the counit
325	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}. It is the base case
326	  (\(m = 0\)) of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{definition}\leanok
330	[Right unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
331	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
333	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
334	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
335	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical right-unitor
336	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X
337	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
338	  \cref{def:unitModule}), the sheafification of the presheaf right unitor
339	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) composed with the counit
340	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{definition}\leanok
344	[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
345	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
347	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,
348	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
349	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical
350	  braiding isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
351	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}\)
352	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), obtained by sheafifying
353	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) the symmetric braiding of the presheaf
354	  monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). It is the
355	  symmetry used in the inductive step of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
356	\end{definition}
357	
358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
366	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
368	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
369	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
370	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
371	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
372	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
373	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
374	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
379	  \leanok
380	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
381	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
382	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
383	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
384	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
385	  equivalence.
386	\end{proof}
387	
388	\begin{lemma}\leanok
389	[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
390	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
392	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
393	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
394	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
395	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
396	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
397	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
398	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
399	\end{lemma}
400	
401	\begin{proof}
402	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
403	  \leanok
404	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
405	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
406	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
407	\end{proof}
408	
409	\begin{lemma}\leanok
410	[Sheafification inverts the localization unit]
411	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
413	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
414	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the sheafification
415	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
416	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of
417	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
418	\end{lemma}
419	
420	\begin{proof}
421	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
422	  \leanok
423	  Feed the abelian local-isomorphism statement
424	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
425	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.
426	\end{proof}

428	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
429	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
430	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
431	  \mathlibok
432	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
433	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
434	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
435	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
436	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
437	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
438	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
439	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
440	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
441	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
442	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
443	\end{lemma}
444	
445	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
446	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
448	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
449	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
450	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
451	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
452	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
453	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
454	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
455	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
456	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
457	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
458	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
459	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
460	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
461	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
462	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
463	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac}
464	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
465	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
466	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
467	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
468	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
469	  \[
470	    a_L,\, a_R :
471	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
472	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
473	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
474	  \]
475	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
476	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
477	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
478	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
479	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
480	\end{lemma}
481	
482	\begin{proof}
483	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
484	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
485	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
486	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
487	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
488	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
489	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
490	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
491	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
492	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
493	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
494	
495	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
496	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
497	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
498	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
499	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
500	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
501	
502	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
503	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
504	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
505	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
506	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
507	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
508	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
509	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
510	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
511	  colimit cofork.
512	
513	\end{proof}
514	
515	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
516	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
517	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
518	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
519	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
520	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
521	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
522	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
523	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
524	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
525	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
526	  parallel pair
527	  \[
528	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
529	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
530	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
531	  \]
532	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
533	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
534	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
535	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
536	\end{lemma}
537	
538	\begin{proof}
539	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
540	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
541	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
542	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
543	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
544	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
545	  three steps.
546	
547	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
548	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
549	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
550	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
551	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
552	  \[
553	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
554	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
555	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
556	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
557	  \]
558	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
559	
560	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families assemble into
561	  morphisms of abelian-group presheaves
562	  \[
563	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
564	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
565	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
566	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
567	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
568	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
569	  \]
570	  i.e.\ natural transformations of functors
571	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\). Naturality of
572	  \(a_L, a_R, \pi\) in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the
573	  restriction maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\): the restriction
574	  of \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection,
575	  because the relative tensor is functorial in \(U\). Colimits in a functor
576	  category are computed objectwise --- by
577	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
578	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
579	  coequalizers of step 1 assemble into a coequalizer of the displayed parallel
580	  pair in \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\).
581	
582	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
583	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
584	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
585	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
586	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
587	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
588	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
589	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
590	  along this identification exhibits
591	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
592	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
593	\end{proof}
594	
595	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
596	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
597	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
598	  \mathlibok
599	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
600	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
601	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
602	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
603	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
604	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
605	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
606	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
607	  category are computed objectwise.
608	\end{lemma}
609	
610	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise value of the presheaf tensor product]
611	  \label{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
612	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_obj}
613	  \mathlibok
614	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
615	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
616	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
617	  commutative rings and \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\) presheaves of modules over
618	  \(R\). For every object \(U\) the value of their monoidal product
619	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is the objectwise relative tensor
620	  product of modules,
621	  \[
622	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
623	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
624	  \]
625	  with the restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps.
626	\end{lemma}
627	
628	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
629	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
631	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
632	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
633	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
634	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
635	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
636	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
637	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
638	  the sheafification adjunction
639	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
640	  presheaves of modules, and write
641	  \[
642	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
643	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
644	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
645	  \]
646	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
647	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
648	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
649	  \[
650	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
651	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
652	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
653	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
654	  \]
655	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
656	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
657	\end{lemma}
658	
659	\begin{proof}
660	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
661	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
662	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
663	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
664	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
665	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
666	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
667	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
668	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
669	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
670	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
671	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
672	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
673	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
674	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
675	
676	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
677	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
678	  is the objectwise relative tensor
679	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
680	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
681	  maps
682	  \[
683	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
684	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
685	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
686	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
687	  \]
688	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
689	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
690	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
691	  pair
692	  \[
693	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
694	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
695	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
696	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
697	  \]
698	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
699	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
700	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
701	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
702	
703	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
704	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
705	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
706	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
707	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
708	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
709	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
710	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
711	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
712	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
713	  comparison maps
714	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
715	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
716	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
717	  isomorphisms.
718	
719	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
720	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
721	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
722	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
723	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
724	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
725	\end{proof}
726	
727	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
728	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
729	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
730	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
731	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
732	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
733	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
734	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
735	  \[
736	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
737	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
738	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
739	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
740	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
741	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
742	  \]
743	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
744	  arguments.
745	\end{corollary}
746	
747	\begin{proof}
748	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
749	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
750	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
751	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
752	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
753	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
754	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
755	  \[
756	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
757	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
758	    \qquad
759	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
760	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
761	  \]
762	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
763	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
764	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
765	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
766	  \[
767	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
768	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
769	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
770	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
771	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
772	  \]
773	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
774	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
775	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
776	  morphism
777	  \[
778	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
779	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
780	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
781	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
782	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
783	  \]
784	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
785	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
786	  \[
787	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
788	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
789	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
790	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
791	  \]
792	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
793	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
794	  composite
795	  \[
796	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
797	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
798	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
799	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
800	  \]
801	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
802	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
803	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
804	\end{proof}
805	
806	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
807	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
808	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
809	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
810	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
811	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
812	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
813	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
814	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
815	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
816	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
817	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
818	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
819	  %  (formulation omitted)."
820	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
821	  power isomorphisms).}
822	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
823	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
824	  \[
825	    \mu_{m,m'} :
826	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
827	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
828	  \]
829	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
830	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
831	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
832	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
833	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
834	\end{lemma}
835	
836	\begin{proof}
837	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
838	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
839	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
840	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
841	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
842	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
843	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
844	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
845	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
846	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
847	
848	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
849	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
850	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
851	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
852	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
853	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
854	  sheaf tensor products
855	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
856	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
857	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
858	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
859	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
860	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
861	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
862	  of the tensor product is never used.
863	
864	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
865	  \(m = 0\),
866	  \[
867	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
868	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
869	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
870	  \]
871	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
872	  recursive definition
873	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
874	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
875	  \[
876	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
877	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
878	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
879	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
880	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
881	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
882	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
883	  \]
884	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
885	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
886	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
887	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
888	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
889	  \[
890	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
891	      \mathcal{L}
892	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
893	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
894	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
895	  \]
896	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
897	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
898	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
899	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
900	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
901	
902	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
903	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
904	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
905	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
906	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
907	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
908	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
909	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
910	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
911	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
912	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
913	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
914	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
915	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
916	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
917	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
918	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
919	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
920	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
921	\end{proof}
922	
923	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
924	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
925	
926	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
927	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
928	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
929	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
930	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
931	global sections.

933	\begin{definition}\leanok
934	[Section multiplication]
935	  \label{def:sectionMul}
936	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
937	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
938	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
939	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
940	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
941	  \[
942	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
943	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
944	    \;\longrightarrow\;
945	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
946	  \]
947	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
948	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
949	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
950	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
951	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
952	  the sheafification unit
953	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
954	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
955	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
956	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
957	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
958	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
959	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
960	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
961	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
962	\end{definition}
963	
964	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
965	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
966	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
967	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
968	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
969	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
970	  the two composites
971	  \[
972	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
973	    \longrightarrow
974	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
975	    \cong
976	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
977	  \]
978	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
979	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
980	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
981	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
982	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
983	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
984	\end{lemma}
985	
986	\begin{proof}
987	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
988	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
989	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
990	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
991	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
992	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
993	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
994	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
995	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
996	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
997	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
998	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
999	  section multiplications.
1000	\end{proof}

1003	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1004	
1005	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1006	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1007	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1008	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1009	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.

1011	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1012	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}

1014	  \mathlibok
1015	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1016	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1017	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1018	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1019	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1020	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1021	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1022	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1023	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1024	\end{lemma}

1026	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
1027	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
1028	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
1029	  \mathlibok
1030	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}

1032	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
1033	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1034	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
1035	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
1036	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
1037	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
1038	  extension of the graded action.
1039	\end{lemma}

909	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
910	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
911	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
912	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
913	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
914	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).

917	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
918	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
919	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
920	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
921	  \(A_g\).

923	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
924	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
925	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
926	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
927	
928	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
929	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
930	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
931	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
932	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
933	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by

979	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
980	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
981	
982	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
983	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
984	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
985	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
986	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
987	
988	\begin{lemma}\leanok
989	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
990	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
991	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
993	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
997	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1001	\end{lemma}
1002	\begin{proof}
1003	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1004	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1010	\end{proof}
1011	
1012	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1013	[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1014	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1016	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1017	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1018	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1019	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1020	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1021	\end{lemma}
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1024	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1025	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1026	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1027	\end{proof}

1029	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1030	[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1031	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1033	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1034	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1035	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1036	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1037	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1038	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1039	\end{lemma}
1040	\begin{proof}
1041	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1042	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1043	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1044	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1045	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1046	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1047	  these conditions gives the claim.
1048	\end{proof}

1050	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1051	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1052	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1053	  \mathlibok
1054	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1055	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1056	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1057	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1058	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1059	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).

1062	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1063	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1064	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1065	  \mathlibok
1066	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1067	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1068	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1069	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1070	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1071	\end{lemma}
