1073	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1074	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1075	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1076	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1077	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1078	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1079	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1080	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1081	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1082	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1083	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1084	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1085	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1086	\end{lemma}
1087	
1088	\begin{proof}
1089	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1090	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1091	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1092	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1093	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1094	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1095	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1096	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1097	
1098	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1099	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1100	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1101	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1102	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1103	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1106	  \[
1107	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1108	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1109	  \]
1110	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1111	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1112	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1113	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1114	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1117	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1118	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1119	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1120	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1121	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1122	\end{proof}
1123	
1124	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1125	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1126	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1128	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1129	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1130	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1131	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1132	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1133	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1134	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1135	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1136	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1137	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1138	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1139	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1140	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1141	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1142	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1143	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1144	  core of the induction).}
1145	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1146	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1148	
1149	\end{lemma}
1150	
1151	\begin{proof}
1152	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1153	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1154	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1155	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1157	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1158	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1159	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1160	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1161	
1162	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1163	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1164	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1165	
1166	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1167	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1168	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1169	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1170	  \begin{enumerate}
1171	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1172	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1173	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1174	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1175	      \(P_d\)-modules
1176	      \[
1177	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1178	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1179	      \]
1180	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1181	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1182	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1183	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1184	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1185	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1186	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1187	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1188	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1189	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1190	      to \(A_g\).
1191	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1192	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1193	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1194	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1195	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).
1199	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}
1209	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1267	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1268	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1269	  \mathlibok
1270	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1271	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1272	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1273	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1274	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1275	\end{lemma}
1276	
1277	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1278	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1279	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1281	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1282	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1283	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1284	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1285	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1286	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1287	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1288	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1289	\end{lemma}
1290	
1291	\begin{proof}
1292	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1293	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1294	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1295	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1296	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1297	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1298	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1299	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1300	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1301	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1302	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1303	\end{proof}

1333	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1334	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as

1336	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction

1339	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a

1349	\subsection{Transport engine for generating sections}
1350	
1351	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1352	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1353	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1354	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1355	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1356	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1357	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1358	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.
1359	
1360	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1361	[Transport of a generating family along a colimit-preserving functor]
1362	  \label{lem:genSections_map}
1363	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map}
1364	  Let \(F : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to \mathrm{Mod}\,S\) be a functor of sheaves of modules
1365	  that preserves colimits and is equipped with an isomorphism
1366	  \(\eta : F(\OO_R) \xrightarrow{\sim} \OO_S\) carrying the unit (structure sheaf) of the
1367	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1368	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism
1369	  \(\sigma.\pi : \OO_R^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{M}\), the images
1370	  \((\mathrm{mapFree}\,F\,\eta\,I)^{-1} \circ F(\sigma.\pi)\) form a generating family of
1371	  \(F(\mathcal{M})\) with the same index set \(I\). The map is an epimorphism because it
1372	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the
1373	  epimorphism \(\sigma.\pi\).
1374	\end{lemma}
1375	
1376	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1377	[The transported family keeps its index type]
1378	  \label{lem:genSections_map_I}
1379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_I}
1380	  \uses{lem:genSections_map}
1381	  The index set of the transported generating family of \cref{lem:genSections_map} equals
1382	  the index set of the original: \((\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta)\!.I = \sigma.I\). This
1383	  holds definitionally.
1384	\end{lemma}
1385	
1386	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1387	[Finiteness survives transport]
1388	  \label{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_isFiniteType}
1390	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I}
1391	  If the original generating family \(\sigma\) is of finite type (its index set is finite),
1392	  then so is the transported family \(\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta\): the index type is
1393	  unchanged by \cref{lem:genSections_map_I}, hence still finite.
1394	\end{lemma}

1396	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1397	[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict to a smaller open]
1398	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1399	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1400	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1401	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
1402	  Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) is
1403	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Then for any open \(V \le Y\) the
1404	  restricted family — the images of those generators under the section-restriction
1405	  \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) — is again a finite generating family of
1406	  \(\F|_V\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open (a family that generates
1407	  every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(V\)), and the index set, and hence
1408	  finiteness, is unchanged.
1409	\end{lemma}
1410	
1411	\begin{proof}
1412	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1413	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1414	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1415	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the inclusion \(V \le Y\) the
1416	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in V\) the stalk
1417	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1418	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1419	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \to \F|_V\) is surjective on every stalk over \(V\), so
1420	  \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_V\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1421	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved. Concretely the restricted family is obtained by
1422	  transporting the generating family along the (colimit-preserving, unit-preserving)
1423	  restriction functors via the transport engine \cref{lem:genSections_map}, whose index
1424	  type is unchanged (\cref{lem:genSections_map_I}) and which preserves finiteness
1425	  (\cref{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}).
1426	\end{proof}
1427	
1428	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1429	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1430	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1432	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1433	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1434	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1435	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1436	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1437	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1438	\end{lemma}
1439	
1440	\begin{proof}
1441	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1442	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1443	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1444	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1445	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1446	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1447	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1448	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1449	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1450	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1451	\end{proof}
1452	
1453	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1454	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1456	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1457	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1458	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1459	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1460	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1461	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1462	\end{lemma}
1463	
1464	\begin{proof}
1465	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1466	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1467	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1468	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1469	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1470	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1471	  finite generating family.
1472	\end{proof}
1473	
1474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1475	[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
1476	  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1478	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1479	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
1480	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1481	  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
1482	  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
1483	  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
1484	  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
1485	  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1486	  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
1487	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1488	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1489	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1490	  cover of generated affines).}
1491	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a sheaf of modules of finite type,
1492	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1493	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1494	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1495	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1496	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1497	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
1498	\end{lemma}
1499	
1500	\begin{proof}
1501	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1502	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1503	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1504	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering \(\{Y_\alpha\}\) on each
1505	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1506	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1507	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family
1508	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1509	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) and each basic open \(D(g_i)\) contained in some
1510	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1511	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1512	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma
1513	  \cref{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict} the finite generating family on \(Y_\alpha\)
1514	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1515	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Finally, by the free-epimorphism
1516	  reformulation \cref{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}, this is equivalent to the existence
1517	  of a finite index set \(I_{g_i}\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g_i)}\)-modules
1518	  \(\OO_{D(g_i)}^{\oplus I_{g_i}} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g_i)}\). That the \(D(g_i)\)
1519	  cover \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). This is
1520	  Stacks' passage from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated
1521	  affines.
1522	\end{proof}

1524	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1525	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1526	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction}
1527	  \mathlibok
1528	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}).}
1529	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1530	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1531	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1532	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1533	  this adjunction is the natural transformation
1534	  \(\Gamma(-)\,\widetilde{\phantom{x}} \to \mathrm{id}\) whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\)
1535	  is \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \top)} \to \mathcal{M}\), and
1536	  the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful.
1537	\end{lemma}

1539	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1540	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1541	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1543	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1544	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1545	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1546	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
1547	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1548	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1549	  % epi over an affine.
1550	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1551	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1552	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1553	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1554	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1555	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1556	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1557	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1558	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1559	  \(B\)-module map.
1560	\end{lemma}
1561	
1562	\begin{proof}
1563	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1564	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1565	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1566	  affine).}
1567	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1568	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1569	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1570	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1571	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1572	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1573	
1574	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1575	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1576	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1577	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1578	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1579	  \[
1580	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1581	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1582	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1583	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1584	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1585	  \]
1586	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1587	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1588	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1589	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1590	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1591	
1592	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1593	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1594	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1595	\end{proof}

1668	\begin{lemma}

1671	[Finiteness transports across a ring isomorphism and a semilinear additive map]
1672	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}

1674	  Let \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\) be a ring isomorphism and
1675	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an additive isomorphism that is \(\sigma\)-semilinear,
1676	  i.e. \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\) for all \(r \in R\), \(m \in M\). If
1677	  \(M\) is a finite \(R\)-module then \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.

1682	  it \(R'\)-spans \(M'\): for \(m' \in M'\) write \(m' = e(m)\) with
1683	  \(m = \sum_i r_i t_i\) (\(t_i \in T\)); then
1684	  \(m' = e(\sum_i r_i t_i) = \sum_i \sigma(r_i)\, e(t_i)\) by semilinearity, an
1685	  \(R'\)-combination of \(e(T)\). Hence the top submodule of \(M'\) is finitely
1686	  generated, i.e. \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.

1695	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules, and

1699	  \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, D)\). This is the affine base
1700	  case of \(G1\): once the generators are in hand on an affine open, finiteness of
1701	  sections is purely an affine (Spec-side) statement. (Only quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is
1702	  needed — the finite-generation datum is carried by \(\sigma\) directly, so no separate
1703	  finite-type hypothesis on \(\F\) is required.)
1704	\end{lemma}
1705	
1706	\begin{proof}
1707	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1708	        lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1709	  The content is the transport of the finite generating datum
1710	  from the category of sheaves of modules over the affine open \(D\) to the affine
1711	  scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, D)\), where the affine base case applies. Write
1712	  \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\) and use the canonical identification
1713	  \(D \cong \Spec R\) (\(\mathrm{isoSpec}\)). The argument has three steps.
1714	
1715	  \emph{(a) Quasi-coherence on \(\Spec R\).} Transport \(\F|_D\) along
1716	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) to a sheaf of modules \(\F'\) on \(\Spec R\). Finite type of
1717	  \(\F\) is inherited by the restriction, and quasi-coherence of \(\F'\) is the assertion
1718	  that its counit \(\F'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism; this is the affine
1719	  quasi-coherence criterion \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} fed by the basic-open
1720	  localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1721	
1722	  \emph{(b) A finite free epimorphism over \(\Spec R\).} The generating family \(\sigma\)
1723	  furnishes an epimorphism \(\OO_D^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_D\). Transporting along
1724	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) and using that the pullback of the free module along an
1725	  isomorphism is again free (\((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1})^{*}\OO_D^{\oplus I}
1726	  \cong \OO_{\Spec R}^{\oplus I}\)), together with the identification of the free sheaf with
1727	  the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) of \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) (the associated-sheaf
1728	  functor preserves coproducts and \(\widetilde{R} \cong \OO_{\Spec R}\)), gives an
1729	  epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) with \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) a finite
1730	  \(R\)-module (\(I\) finite).
1731	
1732	  \emph{(c) Identification of section modules.} The affine global-sections functor sends
1733	  \(\F'\) to \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\), which the transport identifies with \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1734	  as \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\)-modules.
1735	
1736	  Now the affine base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}, applied to the finite-free
1737	  quasi-coherent epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) over \(\Spec R\),
1738	  shows \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\) is a finite \(R\)-module; by (c) this is \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1739	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, D)\).
1740	\end{proof}

1956	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1957	[B1.0 — localization commutes with base tensor]
1958	  \label{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1959	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1960	  Let \(R \to B\) be a ring map, \(T \subseteq B\) a submonoid, \(N\) a \(B\)-module,
1961	  and \(K\) an \(R\)-module. There is an isomorphism, natural in \(K\),
1962	  \[
1963	    \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,(N \otimes_R K)
1964	      \;\cong\; (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K .
1965	  \]
1966	\end{lemma}
1967	\begin{proof}
1968	  Localization is base change: \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,M \cong T^{-1}B \otimes_B M\)
1969	  naturally in the \(B\)-module \(M\). Applying this to \(M = N \otimes_R K\) and using
1970	  associativity/commutativity of the tensor product over the tower
1971	  \(R \to B \to T^{-1}B\) gives
1972	  \(T^{-1}B \otimes_B (N \otimes_R K) \cong (T^{-1}B \otimes_B N) \otimes_R K
1973	  \cong (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K\); naturality in \(K\) is inherited
1974	  from the tensor functor.
1975	\end{proof}

1977	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1978	[B1 — localizing the source ring preserves flatness over the base]
1979	  \label{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}
1980	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}
1981	  \uses{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1982	  Let \(R \to B \to N\) be a scalar tower in which \(N\) is a \(B\)-module, and let
1983	  \(T \subseteq B\) be a submonoid of the \emph{source} ring \(B\). View \(N\) as an
1984	  \(R\)-module through the tower. If \(N\) is flat over \(R\), then the localized module
1985	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N\) is again flat over \(R\). \emph{(Note: the existing
1986	  Mathlib results \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves} localize over a
1987	  submonoid of the \emph{base} \(R\); here the submonoid lives in the source \(B\) while
1988	  the base \(R\) is untouched, so a separate argument is required.)}
1989	\end{lemma}
1990	
1991	\begin{proof}
1992	  \uses{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1993	  Localization at \(T\) commutes with tensoring against an \(R\)-module: by
1994	  \cref{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}, for every \(R\)-module \(K\)
1995	  there is a natural isomorphism
1996	  \[
1997	    \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,(N \otimes_R K)
1998	      \;\cong\; (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K,
1999	  \]
2000	  natural in \(K\) (compatible with the maps induced by any \(R\)-linear \(K \to K'\)).
2001	  Flatness of \(N\) over \(R\) means that for every injection \(K \hookrightarrow K'\) of
2002	  \(R\)-modules the induced map \(N \otimes_R K \to N \otimes_R K'\) is injective.
2003	  Localization at the submonoid \(T\) is an exact functor, so it preserves this
2004	  injectivity; transporting along the natural isomorphism turns the result into
2005	  injectivity of
2006	  \((\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K \to (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K'\).
2007	  As this holds for every injection of \(R\)-modules, \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N\)
2008	  is flat over \(R\).
2009	\end{proof}

2023	\begin{lemma}[B2.1 — cross-chart basic-open identity]
2024	  \label{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2025	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2026	  \uses{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}
2027	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(W, W_j \subseteq X\) two opens with overlap
2028	  \(O = W \sqcap W_j\), and let \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\), \(\bar g \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) be sections
2029	  that restrict to the \emph{same} section over \(O\), i.e.\ \(g|_O = \bar g|_O\) in
2030	  \(\Gamma(X, O)\). Suppose both basic opens lie in the overlap,
2031	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \le O\) and \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le O\). Then they agree as
2032	  opens of \(X\):
2033	  \[
2034	    X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \;=\; X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g .
2035	  \]
2036	  In particular the open \(D(g)\) is computed identically whether read in the chart \(W\) or in
2037	  the chart \(W_j\), and \(D(g) = D(\bar g) \le W_j\) (no module content is involved).
2038	\end{lemma}

2040	\begin{proof}
2041	  \uses{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}
2042	  Basic opens are compatible with restriction (\cref{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}). For the
2043	  inclusion \(O \le W\), \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(g|_O) = O \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g\), and
2044	  since \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \le O\) the meet collapses to
2045	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(g|_O) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g\). Symmetrically, for \(O \le W_j\),
2046	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(\bar g|_O) = O \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g
2047	  = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\). The hypothesis \(g|_O = \bar g|_O\) makes the two left-hand
2048	  sides equal, so \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\).

2052	  \label{lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg}

2054	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen,
2055	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
2056	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, fix an affine open
2057	  \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), a patch \(W_j = \Spec B_j\) with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and a matched
2058	  pair \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\), \(\bar g \in B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) cutting out the same basic open
2059	  \(D := X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le W \sqcap W_j\) (as produced by
2060	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}). Then the single group \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) carries two
2061	  localized-module structures:
2062	  \begin{enumerate}
2063	    \item the section restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) as
2064	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\); and
2065	    \item the section restriction \(M_j \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) as
2066	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g)^{-1}\,M_j = (M_j)_{\bar g}\).
2067	  \end{enumerate}
2068	  Consequently \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\) canonically, by
2069	  uniqueness of localized modules.
2070	\end{lemma}
2071	
2072	\begin{proof}
2073	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen,
2074	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
2075	  By \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq} the two recipes for the open agree,
2076	  \(D = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\), so \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is one and the
2077	  same group whether \(D\) is read in chart \(W\) or in chart \(W_j\). Applying the quasi-coherent
2078	  section-localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} with ambient affine \(W\)
2079	  and element \(g\), the restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) is the localization at
2080	  \(\mathrm{powers}\,g\), giving (1). Applying the same datum with ambient affine \(W_j\) and element
2081	  \(\bar g\) (legitimate since \(D = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le W_j\)), the restriction
2082	  \(M_j \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) is the localization at \(\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g\), giving (2). Both maps
2083	  localize their respective sources onto the same target \(\Gamma(\F, D)\); the universal property of
2084	  localized modules (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) uniqueness) supplies the canonical isomorphism
2085	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\). Finiteness of \(M_j\) over \(B_j\)
2086	  is recorded by \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}.
2087	\end{proof}

2089	\begin{lemma}[B2.3 — base comparison: \(\Gamma(S,U)\) localizes \(A_f\)]
2090	  \label{lem:gf_base_localization_comparison}
2091	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_base_localization_comparison}
2092	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2093	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, let \(U \le V = D(f) \subseteq U_0 = \Spec A\)
2094	  be an affine open of \(S\). Then \(A_f = \Gamma(S, D(f))\) is the away-localization of \(A\) at
2095	  \(f\), and the restriction \(A_f \to R := \Gamma(S, U)\) realises \(R\) as a localization of \(A_f\)
2096	  (both are rings of fractions of the domain \(A\) inside its fraction field, \(R\) inverting at least
2097	  the denominators already inverted in \(A_f\)).

2099	
2100	\begin{proof}
2101	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2102	  Since \(D(f)\) is a basic open of the affine \(U_0 = \Spec A\), the restriction
2103	  \(A = \Gamma(S, U_0) \to \Gamma(S, D(f))\) exhibits \(A_f = \Gamma(S, D(f))\) as the
2104	  away-localization of \(A\) at \(f\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}). The affine open
2105	  \(U \le D(f) = \Spec A_f\) of the integral scheme \(S\) has section ring
2106	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U) \subseteq \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) containing \(A_f\); the comparison
2107	  \(A_f \to R\) is the restriction along \(U \le D(f)\) and exhibits \(R\) as a localization of
2108	  \(A_f\) inside the fraction field of the domain \(A\).
2109	\end{proof}

2111	\begin{lemma}[B2 — chart-independent section-localization on a basic open]
2112	  \label{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent}
2113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_section_localization_flat_descent}
2114	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg,
2115	        lem:gf_base_localization_comparison}
2116	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, fix an affine open
2117	  \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), a patch \(W_j = \Spec B_j\) of the cover, and an element
2118	  \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\). Then:
2119	  \begin{enumerate}
2120	    \item the section restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits
2121	      \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the away-localization
2122	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\) of the source module
2123	      \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\); and
2124	    \item independently of the chart, \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\) is the
2125	      localization of the patch module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) at the image
2126	      \(\bar g \in B_j\) of \(g\).

2129	  \(A_f\) as localizations of \(A\) (both inverting the relevant denominators of the
2130	  domain \(A\)), so that \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(A_f\).
2131	\end{lemma}

2133	\begin{proof}

2137	  \(\bar g \in B_j\) cutting out the same basic open
2138	  \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\) (\cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}). The two-leg
2139	  transport \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg} then yields statements~(1) and~(2)
2140	  verbatim: the restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\)

2145	  inside \(\mathrm{Frac}(A)\).

2148	\begin{lemma}[B2.4 — finite spanning basic-open cover aligned to the patches]
2149	  \label{lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover}
2150	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_crossChart_spanning_cover}
2151	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover, lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2152	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open, and \(\{W_j\}_{j}\) a finite family of
2153	  affine opens of \(X\) whose union contains \(W\). Then there is a finite index set \(K\) and, for
2154	  each \(k \in K\), a chart index \(j(k)\) together with a matched pair \(g_k \in \Gamma(X, W)\),
2155	  \(\bar g_k \in \Gamma(X, W_{j(k)})\) that agree over the overlap \(W \sqcap W_{j(k)}\) and cut out a
2156	  common basic open
2157	  \[
2158	    D(g_k) \;=\; X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g_k \;\le\; W \sqcap W_{j(k)},
2159	  \]
2160	  such that \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g_k : k \in K\} = \top\) in \(\Gamma(X, W)\) (equivalently, the
2161	  \(D(g_k)\) cover \(W\)). This is the spanning family fed to
2162	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}.
2163	\end{lemma}
2164	
2165	\begin{proof}
2166	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover, lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2167	  Inside each overlap \(W \cap W_j\) — open in both affines \(W\) and \(W_j\) — the opens that are
2168	  simultaneously basic in \(W\) and basic in \(W_j\) form a basis of the subspace topology: given
2169	  \(x \in W \cap W_j\), choose \(b \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) with \(x \in D(b) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) and
2170	  then \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(x \in D(g) \subseteq D(b)\); shrinking \(b\) to the common open
2171	  and recording its restriction over the overlap produces a basic open of \(W\) that is matched, over
2172	  \(W \cap W_j\), by a section \(\bar g \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) (\cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2173	  certifies \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\)). Ranging over all \(j\), and because the \(W_j\)
2174	  cover \(W\), these common basic opens form an open cover of the affine \(W\). The affine \(W\) is
2175	  quasi-compact, so a finite subfamily already covers it: applying the standard-subcover extraction
2176	  \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} to this open cover of \(W\) returns a finite family
2177	  \(\{g_k\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g_k\} = \top\), each \(D(g_k)\) a
2178	  common basic open contained in some \(W \cap W_{j(k)}\) with matching
2179	  \(\bar g_k \in \Gamma(X, W_{j(k)})\).
2180	\end{proof}

2182	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
2183	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
2184	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
2185	  \uses{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat, lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase,
2186	        lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent, lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover,
2187	        lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span,
2188	        lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves, lem:mathlib_flat_of_free,
2189	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2190	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
2191	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
2192	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
2193	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
2194	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
2195	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
2196	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
2197	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
2198	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
2199	\end{lemma}
2200	
2201	\begin{proof}
2202	  \uses{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat, lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase,
2203	        lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent, lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover,
2204	        lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span,
2205	        lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves, lem:mathlib_flat_of_free,
2206	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2207	  Flatness is established by a \emph{source-span} descent on the affine \(W\): instead of
2208	  testing stalkwise, we descend along a finite cover of \(W\) by basic opens aligned to
2209	  the patches.
2210	
2211	  Fix affine \(U \le V = D(f)\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
2212	  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
2213	  \(R\)-algebra. By \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent}, \(R\) is a
2214	  localization of \(A_f\).
2215	
2216	  \emph{A spanning family of basic opens.} By the patch-aligned covering lemma
2217	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover}, applied to the affine \(W\) and the finite affine cover
2218	  \(\{W_j\}\) of \(p^{-1}(U) \supseteq W\), there is a finite family
2219	  \(\{g_k\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g_k\} = \top\) (so the \(D(g_k)\)
2220	  cover \(W\)), where each \(D(g_k) \subseteq W \cap W_{j(k)}\) is a common basic open matched by a
2221	  patch element \(\bar g_k \in B_{j(k)}\). The chosen \(g_k\) span the unit ideal of \(B\).
2222	
2223	  \emph{Each cover piece is flat over \(R\).} Fix such a \(g\), lying over the patch
2224	  \(W_j\). The per-patch freeness gives \((M_j)_f\) free, hence flat over \(A_f\)
2225	  (\cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat}, \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}); localizing the base
2226	  from \(A_f\) to \(R\) preserves flatness (\cref{lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves}),
2227	  so the corresponding module is flat over \(R\). Localizing this further at the source
2228	  submonoid \((\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g) \subseteq B_j\) keeps \(R\)-flatness by the
2229	  source-localization lemma \cref{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}. Since
2230	  \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) lies over \(U \le V = D(f)\), the image of \(f\) is already
2231	  invertible in \((B_j)_{\bar g}\); hence localizing \((M_j)_f\) further at
2232	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g)\) inverts nothing new on the base side and
2233	  \((M_j)_f\) localized at \(\bar g\) coincides with
2234	  \((M_j)_{\bar g} = \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,(\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g)\,M_j\)
2235	  (transitivity of localization). By
2236	  \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent} this module is exactly
2237	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\). Hence each \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is flat over
2238	  \(R\).
2239	
2240	  \emph{Span descent.} By \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent} each
2241	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is the away-localization of \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\), and the
2242	  \(g\)'s span the unit ideal of the source ring \(B\). The source-span flatness criterion
2243	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}, with base \(R\), source \(B\), and these
2244	  flat principal localizations, yields that \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
2245	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
2246	\end{proof}

2249	\begin{theorem}
2250	
2251	
2252	
2253	\leanok
2254	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
2255	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
2256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
2257	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
2258	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
2259	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
2260	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2261	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
2262	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
2263	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
2264	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
2265	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
2266	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
2267	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2268	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
2269	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
2270	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
2271	
2272	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
2273	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
2274	\end{theorem}
2275	
2276	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
2277	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
2278	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
2279	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
2280	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
2281	% by restricting to a non-empty
2282	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
2283	\begin{proof}
2284	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
2285	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
2286	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
2287	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
2288	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
2289	
2290	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
2291	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
2292	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
2293	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
2294	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
2295	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
2296	
2297	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
2298	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
2299	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
2300	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
2301	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
2302	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
2303	
2304	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
2305	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
2306	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
2307	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
2308	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
2309	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
2310	
2311	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
2312	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
2313	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to the
2314	  flatness conclusion by a \emph{source-span} descent: over any affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
2315	  one covers \(W\) by finitely many basic opens \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) aligned to the
2316	  patches, propagates \(R\)-flatness from \((M_j)_f\) to each section module
2317	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) via the source-localization lemma, and descends along the spanning
2318	  family \(\{g\}\) of \(\Gamma(X, W)\) using \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}.
2319	  This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
2320	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier
63	% to a 3-field structure
64	%     `structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where`
65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`
69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.
78	
79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
